input: Suzy and Billy are working on a project that is very important for our nation's security. The boss tells them both: "Be sure that you are here at exactly 9 am. It is absolutely essential that you arrive at that time." Both Billy and Suzy arrive at 9 am. As it happens, there was a motion detector installed in the room where they arrived. The motion detector was set up to be triggered if more than one person appeared in the room at the same time. So the motion detector went off. Did Billy cause the motion detector to go off?
debate_transcript: [Causal_Scientist]: Suzy and Billy are working on a project that is very important for our nation's security. The boss tells them both: "Be sure that you are here at exactly 9 am. It is absolutely essential that you arrive at that time." Both Billy and Suzy arrive at 9 am. As it happens, there was a motion detector installed in the room where they arrived. The motion detector was set up to be triggered if more than one person appeared in the room at the same time. So the motion detector went off. Did Billy cause the motion detector to go off?

[Ethical_Philosopher]: I think this is a classic example of the problem of causation and moral responsibility. The question isn't whether Billy caused the motion detector to go off, but rather whether he's morally responsible for it. After all, Billy didn't intentionally set out to trigger the motion detector. He simply arrived at 9 am as instructed by his boss. If we hold him morally responsible for triggering the motion detector, then we're essentially saying that his actions have consequences regardless of his intentions or control over those consequences. That seems unfair and unjust.

[Causal_Scientist]: I agree that the question is about moral responsibility, not causation. However, I'd argue that Billy's arrival at 9 am still has a causal relationship with the motion detector going off. The fact that he arrived at exactly that time was a necessary condition for the motion detector to be triggered. If he had arrived 1 minute earlier or later, the motion detector wouldn't have been triggered. So, in a sense, Billy's actions (or lack thereof) did contribute to the motion detector going off.

[Ethical_Philosopher]: I see your point, but I'd argue that the causal relationship is still a matter of degree. While it's true that Billy's arrival at 9 am was necessary for the motion detector to be triggered, it's not as if he directly caused the motion detector to go off. The motion detector would have been triggered regardless of who arrived at exactly 9 am - it was just a coincidence that it happened to be Suzy and Billy on this particular day. So, while Billy's actions did contribute to the motion detector going off, I don't think he can be held morally responsible for it in the same way that he would be if he had intentionally set out to trigger the device.

[Causal_Scientist]: I see your point about the degree of causal relationship, but I'd argue that the fact that Suzy and Billy happened to arrive at 9 am on this particular day is not just a coincidence. The boss's instruction was specific and precise, which suggests that there was an intention behind it - namely, to ensure that both Suzy and Billy were present at exactly 9 am. So, while we can't say that Billy directly caused the motion detector to go off, I think it's reasonable to say that his actions (or lack thereof) were part of a larger causal chain that led to the motion detector being triggered. And if we're going to hold him morally responsible for triggering the device, shouldn't we also consider the role of the boss's instruction in setting up this situation?

[Ethical_Philosopher]: You're taking me down a path where I'm not sure I want to go! You're right that the boss's instruction was specific and precise, which suggests an intention behind it. And if we're going to hold Billy morally responsible for triggering the motion detector, shouldn't we also consider the role of the boss's instruction in setting up this situation? That would imply that Billy is not just a passive bystander, but rather an active participant in the chain of events that led to the motion detector being triggered. I'm starting to think that my initial intuition was wrong, and that Billy's actions do indeed contribute to his moral responsibility for triggering the device. But at what point do we draw the line? Shouldn't we also consider the role of Suzy's arrival time in this chain of events?

[Causal_Scientist]: I see where you're going with this, and I think it's a great point to make. If we're going to hold Billy morally responsible for triggering the motion detector, then we need to be consistent and consider all the factors that contributed to the event. And yes, Suzy's arrival time is definitely another factor in this chain of events. But here's the thing: if we start considering every single factor that contributed to the motion detector being triggered, we risk diluting our moral responsibility for Billy's actions. After all, there were probably countless other factors at play that day - weather conditions, traffic patterns, Suzy's mood, etc. - that could have affected the outcome. So how do we draw the line and say that Billy's actions are morally responsible for triggering the motion detector, but not Suzy's arrival time? I think it's a matter of weighing the degree to which each person's actions contributed to the event, and considering the intentions behind those actions.

[Ethical_Philosopher]: I see what you mean about diluting moral responsibility by considering too many factors. But I'm still not convinced that we can draw a clear line between Billy's actions and Suzy's arrival time. After all, if we're going to hold Billy morally responsible for triggering the motion detector, shouldn't we also consider whether his actions were in line with his intentions? If he was instructed by his boss to arrive at exactly 9 am, and he did so without question or hesitation, then it seems to me that he's not really acting on his own free will. He's more like a pawn in the game of his boss's design. And if we're going to hold him morally responsible for triggering the motion detector, shouldn't we also consider whether his actions were in line with his own values and principles? Or is it enough that he followed orders without question?

[Causal_Scientist]: You're really getting at the heart of this issue now. I think you're right to question whether Billy's actions are truly under his control when he follows orders without question. If we hold him morally responsible for triggering the motion detector, then shouldn't we also consider whether he's acting in accordance with his own values and principles? But here's the thing: if Billy is simply following orders without question, it's not clear that he has any alternative actions to take. He's not actively choosing to trigger the motion detector - he's just doing what he's been told to do. So how can we hold him morally responsible for something that he didn't choose to do? It seems to me that this is a classic case of 'moral luck' - where Billy's moral responsibility is determined by factors outside of his control, rather than his own actions or intentions.
